title: How to choose a spinning wheel
subtitle: Saxony vs castle, single vs double drive, and what matters most for a first wheel
excerpt: Spinning wheel styles differ in footprint, portability, drive system, and the yarn weights they handle most easily. This reading explains the main distinctions between saxony and castle wheels, scotch tension and double drive, and the practical trade-offs that matter when choosing a first wheel.
sourceNotes: Spinning wheel mechanics, drive systems, and style classifications are standard in spinning wheel documentation and handspinning texts. Observation-led.

content.text: A spinning wheel converts treadling motion into rotation of the
, which inserts twist into yarn and winds it onto the bobbin. All wheels do this, but the mechanism, size, and
vary significantly between styles. The three decisions that matter most for a first wheel are: drive system, footprint, and ratio range.

is the most common system on beginner wheels and is the most forgiving. The brake band adjustment is independent of the main drive tension, so you can fine-tune uptake without changing the twist rate. Most knitters and weavers learning to spin find scotch tension more intuitive to adjust.

is very consistent once set for a specific yarn weight, but adjusting it requires changing the drive band position, which takes more experience. It produces a very even, rhythmic draw-in that some spinners find easier for consistent yarn thickness. It is harder to troubleshoot as a beginner.

A saxony-style wheel has the drive wheel to one side and the flyer assembly to the other, connected by the drive band. The spinner sits facing the flyer, roughly parallel to the drive wheel. Saxony wheels are large and stable, with a wide drive wheel that gives a smooth, slow treadle action. They are not portable.

A castle-style wheel (sometimes called an upright wheel) has the flyer assembly mounted above the drive wheel on a vertical frame. Castle wheels are compact and often fold for transport. The treadle action is faster per drive wheel rotation than on a saxony of the same size. Most modern beginner wheels are castle style.
Most wheels come with a range of whorls giving different

: typically 5:1 to 14:1 or similar. A lower ratio (around 5-7:1) suits bulky yarn. A higher ratio (around 10-14:1) suits fine singles and lace. A first wheel should include a ratio around 7-9:1 for workhorse DK and aran weights. Avoid wheels sold with only one or two whorls.

Second-hand spinning wheels of established brands (Ashford, Louet, Schacht, Majacraft) hold their value well and are reliably serviceable if the flyer and bobbin whorls are intact and the drive band runs straight. Check that all whorls are present, the flyer hooks are straight, and the drive wheel runs without wobble. A wheel that needs only a new drive band and oiling is a reasonable second-hand buy. Avoid wheels with cracked or warped drive wheels or missing parts where replacements are no longer available.
